speaker: interviewer
text: I am Sharon Tyler. And this is tape two. OK, we were talking about moving to Yeshiva and your education there. Well, what was-- tell me a little bit about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeshiva is a traditional way of learning boys Talmud. It was quite a hard regime. You had to get up very early and had to study till the evenings. Friday afternoon and Saturday was a bit easier. But the rest of the week is quite hard learning, though I tried to make-- to mix it with a little bit of playing up. But otherwise, it-- you had to take it seriously. Because after end of the week, you had to-- there was a revision and you had to know what we have learned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still naughty, as you put it, at this age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about how that showed itself when you were in Yeshiva?

words.text: itself when you were in Yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well what-- what I really liked to do, which I'm doing now, I like to-- I like always to pick on on people and teasing them, and especially when I was a youngster. So I was [INAUDIBLE] making fun of everybody under the-- from our seniors as well. I remember that was-- even now I've got that feeling. Sometimes when there is-- when there is a comical situation, somebody looks ridiculous, I cannot help myself not to make fun of him. And sometimes it puts me quite an embarrassing position, when the other one doesn't see the joke of it. But I did that all the time in the Yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Yeshiva you studied at for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was just one year. Because then it started in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would have been-- it would have been how long-- three-- three, four years or however?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normally, I think, looking at my older brothers, their situation, they learned from that age up to about 18, until they start taking some jobs or getting married-- almost to adulthood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your younger brother was following in the sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger brother was still at school at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So in the early years of the war, before Germany came into Hungary, what-- how was your life affected from and how was your life in Kapuvar at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't think it was-- it was all great difference. I mean, still we had the same shabbat meals. And every shabbat, there was a chicken on the table, and fish if they managed to buy the cold fish. It was not like we had a fish shop, which you could buy regular. We had to wait until somebody-- somebody brings in to buy it. The restriction on the trades, on the business, my father's business-- again, that it was in wartime, there is always more opportunities to do business. So as far as financially, I don't think-- I don't think we were so badly off at all. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a restriction on his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, certain-- certain items was not-- they channeled it through non-Jewish distributors. And there were certain things. But we had such loyal customers amongst the non-Jews that even when they had to go somewhere else to buy certain goods, which we are not allowed to trade, for the rest of their goods, they came to us. Because we were giving them such a good service and such honest deals. And we were very popular in our town. And being Abelesz, that was-- that was like-- even anti-Semitic incidents-- I remember us once-- once we were-- a gang of non-Jewish children says that, oh, leave him alone. That's an Abelesz boy. So we had such had a-- such a respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're talking about the wider family were known.

speaker: interviewee
text: The wider family. The wider family. I mean, as I mentioned before, I mean, our family business has been established in the last century. And it was known of honest trading. And they really liked and respected us, the non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father's business was restricted in some ways, but carried on relatively normally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said Friday nights you had chicken and things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. On Friday nights, the Jewish holidays were as before. I mean, Pesach, we had matzos. And we had-- we had let's say-- let's say maybe there was difficulties in the last few years for Sukkot. We couldn't get the [NON-ENGLISH], which a Jewish viewer would understand what that means, that we had to have that [NON-ENGLISH], which is a citrus fruit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would that-- where would you have got it from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think usually that comes from-- either from North Africa or Italy. And I suppose during the war years, they were just not available. Only one or two somehow was in the whole country. And the people had to go to Budapest if they wanted to keep that commandment. But especially end of-- towards 1943, when the war was not working well to Hungary, when the Russians has counter-offended and there was a lot of Hungarian casualties, and the attitude of the government has changed, it-- one, we felt there came a prime minister who was more friendlier to the Jews. And we felt that this is now as far as the evil goes and now it's going to be an improvement. Even they were talking about that Horthy doesn't want to give into Hitler. And we really thought, by 1944, that we will survive and the Hungarian Jews will be saved, until it came that fateful day of 19th of March, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember. I remember it. Just out of the blue, there was-- it is a Sunday. And Germans army marched through the streets. Because that was a-- one of the main routes towards Budapest. And everybody was very worried that our fate is going to be like the rest of Europe. And of course, there were change of the government. The more-- more balanced politicians has been dismissed and has been appointed right wing politicians. And we heard that Horthy, which at that time we looked at him that our savior, the regent [INAUDIBLE] Horthy-- I don't think he was such a great person, such a good person. But he played-- he was-- he knew that the war is not going in favor of the Germans. So he tried to backpedal. But unfortunately, the Germans has arrested him. And the Germans took-- Germans and their helpers, their supporters-- took over the government of Hungary. And then we knew that our fate is sealed. I remember that. I was-- as a child, I was-- I saw the worried faces of my parents and everybody, the whole community. And I knew that we haven't seen-- what we have seen so far is nothing to what is going to come. It was very, very worrying times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, first, I think within a few weeks, there was restrictions. You had to wear yellow stars, all from the age of six years upwards. You have to wear yellow stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they sewn on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sewn on. You have to cut it out from yellow materials. You have to sew on or wear it on your breast. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it mean for you, doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that we should be-- we should be seen as we are Jews. And we knew that this is how it started in Poland and in the other countries. I knew that this is going to be our fate, the same, or similar like it happened to Poland. And the restrictions-- and they-- I think immediately we started-- Jewish shops are to be simply closed. Closed and-- closed down the Jewish shops. Professions-- cannot be Jewish people anymore in professions. It's also where very few Jewish people were in the civil service, but also where in some kind of local government or something, they're instantly dismissed. And we became really second class-- even worse than second class. And then, gradually, as-- in fact, in Hungary, it went quite fast. By May, they said that there will be Jewish houses in our town, in Kapuvar. So the people who lived in the villages around it had to move into Kapuvar. And families, like we were, lived in the center, where it was appointed as a Jewish house, we had to take in another family with us. It happened to be a relation. The Jewish communities, as communities, has been disbanded. And in every town were appointed Judenrats, Jewish council, who has to take instruction directly from the-- from the German authorities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me some more about that? Were some of your family involved in the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was also one of the members of the council.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he-- was that something that was appointed? Did he volunteer for that? Or he was told to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I don't remember. I don't know. Whether he has been appointed or he just volunteered-- but there was-- and really, the Judenrat's job was really to carry out the German orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was not a very-- while it was a position of responsibility, it wasn't very desirable position of respons--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a very desire-- but I mean, this is not a unique case in Hungary. It was everywhere. They always thought, if we are-- and the Germans, always they acted very politely as far as the official capacity. And they said, this is what you have to do. You have to make a list of all the Jewish people, all the Jewish assets, just list and typing and sending into the central all kinds of reports and documentations. And as gradually-- gradually, things got worse. And they have took off the gloves. And then they realized that the Judenrat and everything is being used as an organizing it, that it should be easier for the Germans. But it didn't serve the purpose of the Jewish community. Not that there was any other choice. But that's how it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with your father being involved in that, what-- what was that like? How was it in your family with your father being on the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Possibly he knew more about what's happening than we did. But as far as I know, I haven't seen any difference between us and the others. We were just sitting and waiting what's going to happen. And we didn't have to wait too long. By-- I think it was in a Saturday, when we heard somehow that gendarmeries has arrived in Kapuvar. Because what the Germans did-- not only that they did the dirty work [INAUDIBLE]. They got the Jews with the Hungarians-- they used the Hungarian gendarmerie army to do that. Also, they moved-- moved from one place to another, that those peop-- those who were known to the local population, they don't have to take part in it. But we knew that there is a squad of gendarmerie came to Hungary. And they said that tomorrow morning we are going to be taken away. And it happened. They woke us up early morning, knocked on the door. Pack your belongings. You only take as much as you can carry. And we are going to the school for-- for search, where they-- don't take any jewelry or money you have to declare. And after a long day, we were put into wagons. And we are taken to [? Sopron, ?] which is a large town, into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, just go back up for a second to talk about the-- you said your house became a Jewish house in Kapuvar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your relatives moved in with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moved in with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people moved in with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think a family-- a family of five or six, if my memory's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it became-- the house had also its own yellow star. Did it become sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I don't remember. No, I don't think there was anything outside to mark it from any other houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But all the Jews were brought together, were they? Well, all the Jews brought together for a simple reason-- if it should be easier when it comes to rounding them up, they should-- it should make it easier. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we always thought it's just [INAUDIBLE] that's where we are going to stay. Or at least, that's what we were-- we were trying to explain it away. Because one is always optimistic. And all right, so this not the worst. So we've got another family staying with us. If you can survive like that, we'll be good. But of course, things got always gradually worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came round and they knocked on the doors, you said, and said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you have to take your belongings and march-- join the march outside. Outside the hall, there was already we saw that all the Jewish families were standing middle of the road. And we had to walk to the-- it was quite a distance. Because the school was on the other end of the town. And everybody had to walk there with their miserable belongings. And the general population just stood on both sides of the road. And they just watched us. I remember it. I remember it. It was-- just watching us. I couldn't detect any hostility or any great joy of being taken away. It just like watching something interesting happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you rem--

speaker: interviewee
text: No remarks and no expression of sadness, just looking at us. I remember also another incident. I wrote a postcard, because my brother was already not with us. One of my brothers was called into the labor battalions. And I saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the third one. Because the oldest, my oldest brother, was in Budapest already. They left before the Germans came to Hungary. He lived in Budapest. And I wanted-- I wrote a postcard, which we still got it somewhere. I don't know where, because he got it. And I wanted to post it in the letter box, as it was there-- a mail box. And one of the gendarmerie said to me, no. You mustn't step out on the side. Where you want to go? I said, I just want to post a letter. He said, give it to me. I will post it. And he really posted it. Because-- because he got it. And he said-- he was looking after it until the very end of the war, that postcard which I've written to him, on the 18th of June, when we are marching to the preparation to take us to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people in your home at the time when you were rounded up were your parents, your younger brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: And one older brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who was together with me in Auschwitz, my sister. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two brothers-- two older brothers-- they were already in the labor battalion but not in the same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the great aunt who lived--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just came with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: She came-- she came with you, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you walked to the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember any-- you remember the kind of expressionless-ness of the people around you.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened in the school-- I just don't remember anything clearly. Because possibly the children were not involved. But then they have-- then they have interviewed people. And people-- people [INAUDIBLE]. They got-- they checked everything, whether they've got any jewelries, and taken away whatever valuables you carried-- jewelry and money, these kind of things. And the end of the day, it was a relief. And we were taken into the wagons, into the railway station.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Kapuvar.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Kapuvar. To go to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To go to Sopron.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Sopron.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sopron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything specific that your family took with? I mean, did you take any Judaica with you, or remember what happened to your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I took my [NON-ENGLISH], which I just a year ago-- year before was bar mitzvahed. That was my more precious things. And maybe a prayer book-- I took a prayer book with me. But it's very little what you can carry-- just a bit of food for emergencies. Really very little, because you cannot take more than you can carry-- and beddings. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well tell me something about what you remember about the journey, the train, the cattle and the wagons.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very short journey. Because it's not very far from Sopron. I think we arrived-- we arrived the next morning, I think. Funnily enough, I don't remember much about that journey. All I remember when we were in Sopron is the ghetto. That was a very primitive conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very primitive conditions. It was in a huge buildings. It was the first-- when the provincial Jews were taken to Sopron, we were under-- in a building. I think it was a communal kitchen. And the people lived more than one family in one room, just on the floor you had to sleep. And then-- and then I think we stayed there for about two weeks in that building. Then later on, with the community of the Sopron-- they were taken together. They were already there, in brick fields. That was really horrible. That was-- most of it was on the open air.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brick fields?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brick fields, where they cast-- where they-- that's what it's called.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where they make bricks. So it's just an enclosure. And the conditions were very primitive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the food was provided, the communal kitchen. In fact-- in fact, my sister was telling me-- I didn't remember it at that time-- that somebody from Kapuvar, one of our friends, a non-Jewish friend, came up especially to Sopron to bring us extra food and to bribe the guards to bring it into the ghetto to us, while we were there. We very much appreciated it. Not we were short of food. But because it was plenty of food there. It was all from the Jewish kitchens they got together all the food. But that somebody really cares of us, with-- at a great risk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the communal food was-- you all sort of took turns in cooking? It was all part-- everyone in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was every-- it was cooked together, by mass cooking. I don't know what. And everybody had according to their families. But we had some other food. I don't-- all I know that we were not hungry. That, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not hungry, but primitive. And it was cold, damp, was it? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not cold and damp. Because that's the middle of the summer. That was in July. So as long as-- we didn't suffer from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember about the-- what is it that struck you as most primitive about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were sleeping on the floor-- not even on floor boards, just on the ground, on the earth. And there was no proper facilities. Toilet facilities were non-existent. I think that they made some latrine somewhere. But-- and we were just there, like a mass of humanity. What went on-- they took a lot of people, people who were suspected they were rich people and they must have hidden somewhere, they took them back. And they managed under distress-- great physical violence and torture-- they had to reveal where they got their goods. I know my father, luckily, was not among them, because we were not regarded as rich. But a number of people who came back from-- they took them back to Kapuvar. And they had to show them where they were hiding their goods. Then we realized that this is-- this is terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to move around at all in Sopron when the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- we are enclosed in a ghetto. No, not--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, very enclosed? I mean, was it a walled-- was it a walled ghetto? Or how was-- how was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. It was, yes. It was a walled-- it was not really a ghetto. It was something-- some kind of industrial odd buildings or something. And not everybody was under cover-- covered-- not under roof. We were just being there temporarily. We realized that. We didn't stay there long. And we were just put in the wagons a few days later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Sopron for--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sopron, we were on the two places altogether from the 18th of June to the 5th of July. So it's 12-- it's about less than three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did it come about on the 5th of July? What happened on that date?

speaker: interviewee
text: On 5th of July, we had to walk. They were already worked out that 80 people per wagon. And they have selected. They were already organized which wagon is going to-- who is going to which wagon. And we just were taken there by the SS men accompanied us. I remember Germans-- not the gendarmerie, the Hungarians. And 80 people had to stay in wagon, had to walk up to the wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you with your family at this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And my poor great-aunt-- during the journey, he didn't understand. By that time, she must have been in her late 80s. And she didn't understand what is going on. She kept on saying, I don't want to travel anywhere. I want to get off. That poor soul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Your great-- you were telling me the great aunt on the train said she wanted to get off. What happened? She was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she felt very uncomfortable on the train to Auschwitz. And she kept on saying, look, I don't want to go any further. And I want to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: She must have been mid-80s or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she survived the journey with you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: She survived the journey. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: She made it to--

speaker: interviewee
text: She survived the journey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- was your father, having been on the Judenrat, was he involved in any way in working out who went on which wagon? Or was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I suppose that's how far his authority went. He said that our family and the cousins-- they will stay in the same wagons and put in-- put in for each wagon water, and sanitary stuff, and bread were given to each wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember was your personal experience of that train, of that journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I remember, I had hardly eaten anything. I was not hungry or something. I know-- I know I didn't suffer from hunger or thirst. We are just numbed, just numbed. We are just watching. From that little window, you could see sometimes the name of the stations where we are passing through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you think you were going? Or what did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it was obvious from the direction that we are going to Poland. I remember the last big Hungarian town was [? Kosice. ?] Then we left. And then-- and then some foreign sounding names of towns, all from southern Poland. And then, on the last day of the journey, we came to a very big place, Krakow. I remember that as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stop in any of the-- did the train stop in--

speaker: interviewee
text: The train stopped. The train stopped nearly every-- it took-- I mean, that shouldn't have-- shouldn't have lasted four or five days, such a journey. But it stopped in every places. So why it stopped? Because local condition-- I mean, during the end of the war, there were-- things were not normal as far as the railway station. Or what I am thinking-- that the whole deportation has been organized in such a way, according to the capacity of the gas chambers, that they should be able to-- should arrive gradually, as many people as the capacity is. That's why was-- Hungary was-- the Hungarian Jews were taken gradually.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was all a very well masterminded scheme?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well masterminded scheme, very efficiently organized by Eichmann. And what it took in Poland years to achieve and organize it, when it come to Hungary, they had their experience. And they really organized it very efficiently.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of arriving in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in Auschwitz, there was banging on the wagon doors opened up. They opened it up from the outside. Everybody out. We saw it was on a bright day, bright summer day. We saw like, barbed wires, camps as far as the eye can see. And surrounded us a lot of prisoners, existing prisoners, with the blue striped uniforms. And they said, if you leave everything here, later on it will be distributed. And they have to-- you have to stand separately. Women with children should stand separately. And old people and the men-- separate rows. I remember it-- my younger-- I saw my sister went with my mother. And my younger brother-- I went with my father and my older brother. And my younger brother also wanted to come with us. And I remember I said to him, well, better off you are go with-- with mummy. And then he went with mummy. And then I stood in front, when it came to our row. I stood in front of a German officer, which later on I knew it was Dr. Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he pointed. He looked at me and my brother. And my brother was much taller than me, so it was no problem with him. And he looked at me, asked me, how old are you? In German. I understood German. So I said to him, I'm 14. I just had my birthday, which happened to be true. That week, I had my birthday. So he smiled at me. He says, [GERMAN]. And then he sort of looked at me again. And he pointed at me together with my brother. And my father also wanted to follow us. And he said, no. Not you. You go the other side. So that was divided the people who immediately went to the gas chambers and those people who went into the camp. And did you see your father after that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't see again. Not my parents, not my younger brother-- I didn't seem them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your younger brother who had gone-- who you had said to go with your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your sister had also gone with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And later on-- later on-- I mean, what I found out later on-- that she survived. Later on, I found out that she went-- she was sent also in the camp and survived. And I survived, and my older brother, in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay-- were you with your brother, sort of most of the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, most of the time. Most of the time, I was with my brother, luckily. Because he helped me. He helped me. He helped me. He gave me a lot of encouragement. He always sort of kept an eye on me. And if not him, I don't think I would have survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- what was his name, this brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe. Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Moshe, right. What happened after that initial selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into disinfection shower. And all our clothes-- this has to be left behind. And we were given also similar clothes as like those prisoners were wearing-- some kind of underwear, a cap, and a jacket and trousers. And the shoes-- that you left, the old shoes, yeah. I had a good pair of shoes. I remember it. These were made especially for all of us-- solid, heavy boots. And then after-- afterwards, we are directed marching and into the camp, after the disinfection and shower. And we were taken into barracks, where well, just sitting there numbed. There was nothing what to do. And then we had to learn the routine of the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn that routine?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were told what to do. I mean, we had to wake up in the morning. And in the morning, they have given us dishes-- given us hot drinks. And lunch time was-- was a kind of a vegetable soup. In the evening, we had bread with something. And during the day, it was really nothing much to do. You could walk in the camp, all over the place. But we knew that all the arrivals who came into the camp-- they are not staying there long. Because they were-- they were distributed to various labor camps in Germany. And always new faces-- they are coming and going. Then they arrived-- then they arrived, the Jews from-- from Lodz ghetto, from Poland. And then transports came from-- from Greece. And then we realized it-- after our transport was almost the last of the Hungarian transports. And there were just a few days later came from cities around Budapest. But what we-- we realized it in Auschwitz itself that from Budapest, no transports are coming. And then that we saw that-- Jews of Budapest, which were really a substantial part of the Jewish community in Budapest, at that time must have lived possibly-- the records will say it otherwise. But I just-- between 200,000 or 300,000 Jewish people lived in Budapest. A lot of refugees all accumulated there. But no transports arrived from there to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you surmise from that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they are there, still in Budapest, and which is a good thing. Because gradually, we realized that where we are. We were taken a camp which was called [GERMAN], gypsy camp. Because the original inhabitants were the gypsies there. In fact, one side of the camp, there were still some gypsies there. And after about two weeks of being there we arrived, the gypsies were all rounded up and taken to the gas chambers, those gypsies who were in the camp. Then it dawned on us that there are gas chambers, where they are gassing people. Because some people-- you see, one hears from the other that there gas chambers. And you don't want to believe it. Some people, I think, always ask the older inhabitants of the camps, when can we see our parents, or something, who arrived. They say, no, no. You will have given an opportunity later. But it was all lies. I mean, there was no-- by that time, there was-- those who didn't come into the camps, they were-- they were killed on the same day as they arrived. And when the gypsy camp was evacuated, and they were screaming at the night, and we heard it. And there was thrown belongings. I remember it. They had their blankets. And it was all on the-- we realized that they were taken to the gas chambers. And then we thought, if the gypsies are being killed, we are-- in the Germans' eyes, I don't think we are in a better category than them. Then we realized it that there is-- the fate, what has befallen the people who didn't come, and what fate is going to await for us in the camp.
